articles.title: How Does Baking Powder Work?
articles.description: Baking powder makes batter and dough rise by producing carbon dioxide bubbles. Here is what triggers it, what goes wrong, and how to test your can.
articles.answerSummary: Baking powder combines a dry acid and base that release carbon dioxide after liquid is added; heat expands those bubbles while the batter or dough sets around them.
articles.problemLabel: My baking powder is not working
problemLabels: My baking powder is not working

notes: This mechanism guide was researched from university extension sources; How Biscuit did not run a controlled product test.

items.title: Cooking Food Storage: baking powder activity and double-acting acids
Storing Staple Ingredients in the Kitchen: freshness test
Ingredient Substitutions for Cooking and Baking

steps.title: Liquid starts the reaction
steps.body: Water lets the dry acid and base dissolve and meet. Their reaction releases carbon dioxide into the batter or dough.

steps.title: Bubbles get trapped
steps.body: The gas fills existing air pockets in the mixture. Mixing method and batter structure decide how well those pockets hold.

steps.title: Heat expands, then sets
steps.body: In the oven, gas expands and double-acting powder releases another round. Proteins and starches then set the final structure.

presentationBlocks.title: More baking powder does not mean more height
presentationBlocks.body: Too much can make a bake rise quickly and then collapse before the structure is strong enough to hold it. It can also leave an unpleasant chemical or bitter taste. Measure the recipe amount; the can is not a volume knob.

items.title: The can is tired
items.body: Moisture and time reduce its useful activity.
items.fix: Test a teaspoon in one-third cup of hot water. Vigorous foaming means it still has rising power.

items.title: The batter waited
items.body: The first gas release began at mixing, then escaped while the bowl sat.
items.fix: Preheat the oven and prepare the pan before adding the wet ingredients.

items.title: The amount drifted
items.body: A heaped spoon or a confused substitution changed the acid-base balance.
items.fix: Use a level measuring spoon and keep baking soda substitutions recipe-specific.

items.title: The bubbles got bullied
items.body: Aggressive mixing can damage the structure that needs to hold expanding gas.
items.fix: For tender quick breads and biscuits, mix only until the dry streaks disappear.

presentationBlocks.title: The cheap safe version
presentationBlocks.body: Test the can before buying another one. If it fails, replace it rather than improvising a large amount of baking soda. A small can costs less than a pan of dense, metallic-tasting cake.
